items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: When you push a wall, what does Newton's third law say about the wall?
items.correct: The wall exerts an equal and opposite force on you at the same instant.
items.misconception: The wall only pushes back if your force is strong enough to make it react.
items.random_wrong: The wall pushes back with a force proportional to how stiff it is, not equal to your force.
items.why: Students think action-reaction is causal and sequential — push first, then the wall 'responds' — instead of being two aspects of one interaction.

items.q: What is the difference between mass and weight?
items.correct: Mass is the amount of matter (kg); weight is the gravitational force on that mass (N) and depends on g.
items.misconception: Mass and weight are the same thing, just expressed in different units.
items.random_wrong: Mass changes with location but weight does not.
items.why: Students confuse mass with weight because everyday English uses 'weight' for both, and bathroom scales display mass in kg labelled as 'weight'.

items.q: A 2 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the resultant force?
items.why: It's 8 N. “2 N” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does it mean if the resultant force on an object is zero?
items.correct: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.
items.why: Correct answer: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.. “The object must be stationary.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Calculate the weight in newtons of a 5 kg mass on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg).
items.why: Exact answer: 49 N. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
